narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 39 and an undergraduate GPA of 3.0, you've put together a competitive profile in many ways, including planning to attend law school full-time like the vast majority of students. However, the admissions model predicted a 42% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which crossed the threshold for rejection—meaning the school sees too high a risk that you might not succeed long-term despite your strengths. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is well below the typical student's rank of around the 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school raised concerns about your readiness for the bar's demands. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your LSAT score, which is stronger than the average of 36.7 and pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass by showing sharp analytical skills. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, slightly below the average of 3.21, also helped nudge things in a positive direction but wasn't quite enough to offset the concerns. Additionally, your lower-middle family income bracket factored into the broader context, though it wasn't among the top influences here. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your Year 3 performance stands out as the weakest link, while your test scores align more closely with successful applicants. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or your hard work—it's the model weighing patterns from past students to protect the school's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year rankings, as that could dramatically shift future predictions. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or explore schools with more holistic reviews beyond these metrics. With your LSAT strength as a foundation, focusing on consistent academic improvement could open doors at other programs willing to take a chance on your upside. Keep pushing; many students turn similar profiles around with strategic next steps.
